analysis:
- Common backbone: best heuristics combine a Pareto prefilter, a cheap proxy ranking (box-volume +/- scalarizations), an O(k)-proportional deterministic shortlist, a CELF-style lazy greedy using cached singleton HVs and timestamps, selective pairwise caching for tiny shortlists, then bounded 1-for-1 swap refinement and deterministic padding. That pipeline minimizes expensive hypervolume calls while targeting high-quality candidates.
- Cost/quality knobs: shortlist size, number of weight vectors, pairwise-cache threshold, and HV-call budget dominate runtime vs solution quality tradeoffs. Strongers use moderate shortlists and more careful greedy/CELF logic; weaker ones either do many full HV calls or use heavy randomization and redundant recomputation.
- Efficiency patterns: vectorized proxy computations (ref - pts, products, dot with weight matrices) and explicit caches (singleton HVs, occasional pairwise HVs) drastically reduce Python-level overhead. CELF lazy timestamps avoid recomputing marginals until needed.
- Avoidable overheads seen in weaker heuristics: repeated np.vstack and full-set HV recomputations inside tight loops, linear membership checks (using lists rather than sets), unbounded swap loops, and missing HV-call budgets that blow up runtime.
- Robustness patterns: deterministic tie-breaking, seeded RNG, and deterministic padding prevent nondeterministic outputs. Many heuristics also guard for degenerate inputs and reference-point handling.
- Practical trade: pairwise caching helps only when shortlist ≤ ~30–50; beyond that it costs too many HV calls. Conservative upper bounds (box-volume or singleton HV) let you prune cheaply before exact HV verification.
- Quality improvements often come from slightly larger, better-ranked shortlists and a CELF selection that truly uses cached singleton gains rather than naive per-candidate full recomputation.
experience: Favor a pipeline that (1) vectorizes cheap proxies to form an adaptive O(k) shortlist, (2) precompute singleton HVs, (3) run CELF lazy greedy with timestamps and cheap upper‑bound pruning, (4) limit exact HV calls with an explicit budget and selective pairwise cache, and (5) finish with a tiny bounded swap. Optimize data structures (sets, arrays) and avoid repeated full HV recomputations.
current_reflection: Favor a pipeline that (1) vectorizes cheap proxies to form an adaptive O(k) shortlist, (2) precompute singleton HVs, (3) run CELF lazy greedy with timestamps and cheap upper‑bound pruning, (4) limit exact HV calls with an explicit budget and selective pairwise cache, and (5) finish with a tiny bounded swap. Optimize data structures (sets, arrays) and avoid repeated full HV recomputations.
good_reflections: Prefilter dominated points, approximate HVC with few weight vectors to shortlist candidates, then perform a local search using cached incremental HVC deltas. Minimize Python-side HV calls by batching and avoid nested HV recomputations. Make the algorithm deterministic (seeded) and ensure it always returns exactly k points.

Design next heuristics by enforcing an O(k) shortlist, always cache singleton HVs, use CELF lazy evaluation with timestamps, apply cheap box-volume upper bounds to prune candidates, enable pairwise caching only for very small shortlists, cap HV calls and swap iterations, and prefer deterministic stable ordering. These focused changes yield most HV-quality gain per expensive HV call.

Prioritize reducing exact HV calls: build a high-precision O(k) shortlist with cheap proxies, cache singleton (and small pairwise) HVs, use CELF lazy updates and box‑bound pruning, then run a tiny deterministic swap pass. Add an explicit HV-call budget with graceful fallback approximations and vectorized nondomination/precomputation to get consistent, fast, and high-quality k‑subsets.
bad_reflections: Favor deterministic Pareto prefilter, vectorized box+scalar proxies to build an O(k) shortlist, precompute singleton HVs, run CELF lazy greedy with timestamps, conditionally cache pairs for small shortlists, apply tight cheap upper bounds to prune, cap HV calls, and use efficient numpy masks/sets. These deliver high-quality hypervolume with far fewer expensive HV evaluations.

For the next generation, combine: adaptive shortlist sizing (scale with pareto size and k), CELF lazy greedy with cached singletons + selective pairwise HVs, and tight upper-bound pruning (box-vol or scalar HVC) to avoid useless HV calls. Add an HV-call budget, incremental HV updates, and prioritize swap candidates via proxy gains or diversity. Vectorize dominance checks and weight computations; keep all randomness seeded and tie-break deterministically.

Always budget and minimize HV calls: prioritize vectorized proxies for aggressive pruning, cache singleton HVs, compute pairwise HVs only for very small shortlists, and use CELF lazy timestamps to amortize updates. Favor deterministic, O(N log N) shortlist construction and bounded, incremental swaps. Optimize hot loops with numpy and sets to avoid Python overhead; add an explicit HV-call budget and conservative upper bounds to maximize runtime-quality trade-offs.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: vectorized proxies, O(k) shortlist, singleton HV cache, CELF lazy + timestamps, box‑bound pruning, pairwise cache (tiny), HV‑call budget, bounded swap, deterministic.
- Advice: Build an adaptive O(k) shortlist via vectorized cheap proxies, precompute singleton HVs, run CELF lazy greedy with timestamps and box‑volume upper bounds, enable pairwise HV caching only for very small shortlists, cap exact HV calls and swaps, and finish with a tiny deterministic swap; use arrays/sets and seed RNG.
- Avoid: repeated full HV recomputations, unbounded pairwise caching, nested HV calls, nondeterministic tie breaks, and heavy Python loops in hot paths.
- Explanation: These focused rules minimize expensive HV evaluations while producing stable, high‑quality k‑subsets with predictable runtime.
